name: Battle: i will go for a...

data.content: i will go for a walk
Successfully fighting two opponents simultaneously presents significant tactical disadvantages and force multiplication. This makes an 'easy' victory highly improbable for almost anyone.
what if they are 2 skinny guys and I am a well trained MMA fighter and they dont have any weapons or any help
You're relying on a highly specific, ideal scenario that rarely reflects the unpredictable nature of real-world threats.
but thats a scenario that already happened
The fact it already happened doesn't make it irrelevant; rather, it demands we analyze *why* it occurred and what lessons apply to our current situation.
you are changing the topic you didn't beat me i won the fight for training purposes
Redefining victory as 'for training purposes' *is* changing the topic, as it shifts the goalposts from our original debate's objective, thus avoiding a direct concession on the initial point.
I'm sorry the main point is that i can win the fight doesn't matter the settings of the fight
Your ability to win is inherently contextual; the setting often dictates which strengths are relevant and can even define the parameters of victory.

Your statement "i want to eat 100 burgers" is a personal desire, but it doesn't provide any logical reasoning or consideration for the potential health and nutritional implications of consuming such a large number of burgers.
